text: Abstract The endocrine disrupting chemical Bisphenol A (BPA) is one of the most studied chemicals due to its wide distribution in human everyday life. In response to the negative publicity and legislation restricting the use of BPA, manufacturers have switched to less well studied BPA alternative plastics. Some of these analogues have similar properties to BPA and have been shown to interact at similar sites on nuclear receptors thereby causing similar endocrine disrupting effects to BPA. These effects include binding with agonistic or antagonistic effects to estrogen (ER), androgen (AR), glucocor- ticoid (GR), aryl hydrocarbon (AhR), and pregnane X receptor (PXR) receptors. The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) recently published a draft assessment for Bisphenol A (BPA) following a request by the European Commission to reassess the safe level of BPA for humans. The safe level was drastically lowered from 4 mg/kg body weight/day to 0.2 ng/kg body weight/day due to adverse effects that have been reported such as immune system disruption. As a result, we assessed the current knowledge on the immunomodulatory effects of BPA alternatives plastics. Our analysis suggests that BPA alternatives may affect the innate and acquired immune responses and may therefore lead to adverse health effects such as type I hypersensitivity reactions, allergies or disruption of the human gut microbiota.
explanation: The text uses authentic scientific phrasing and references regulatory framing, making it plausible as human-written; however, the unusual hyphenation ('glucocor- ticoid') and some specific dosage claims could indicate AI generation or rapid editing.
